clean_story: Invisible cosmic glue.  Galaxies held by unseen mass. "Dark matter" describes something we can’t see but feel—like wind pressing against you in total darkness. It doesn’t emit light, absorb light, or reflect it—yet its gravity bends stars’ paths like a hidden hand guiding a spinning top. Here’s the proof:Galaxies rotate too fast. Stars at their edges should fly off like mud from a tire—but they don’t. Something invisible adds extra pull. Same with galaxy clusters:Their light bends around empty space, as if a massive lens floats there. That lens? Dark matter. It outweighs normal matter 5 to 1—like an iceberg where only the tip (stars, planets) is visible. We detect it by subtraction:Measure a galaxy’s gravity, subtract the mass of its visible stars—what’s left is the dark stuff. Best guess?A flood of slow-moving particles, barely interacting, passing through you now like ghosts through walls. Three related subjects are cosmic inflation, black hole math, and neutrino trails.
